summary: O'Neil explains why she and her husband have boycotted watching football after years of fandom. She argues that the accumulated evidence of CTE among players—with studies showing 96% of deceased players tested positive—makes it unconscionable to be entertained by a sport she wouldn't let her own children play. She draws a parallel to military service, noting that privileged families avoid both while others bear the costs. The piece criticizes the NFL's forced patriotism and militaristic rhetoric as a distraction from the real harm being done. She concludes that the violence, the money protecting the status quo, and the absurd intensity around wins and losses have finally made her walk away from the game.
key_points: CTE is nearly universal among football players, with a study finding 96% of deceased NFL players tested positive for the brain disease
There's a moral inconsistency in being entertained by a sport you wouldn't allow your own children to play
The NFL's forced patriotism and military rhetoric serves to distract from addressing the sport's violence problem
Unlike military service, which serves a societal need, nobody needs to play football—making it easier to simply boycott
The intense shame and gravity attached to college football losses is absurdly disproportionate and potentially harmful to young players
money_quotes: I have three sons, and I wouldn't let any of them play football. So what does it mean that I let myself be entertained by other people playing it?
A good approximation of a complete list would be 'all football players, ever.'
One of the most uneasy aspects of my enjoyment of football has always been the way the NFL and even college football coaches and media play up and play to the military aspects of the game.
It makes you think of the worst kinds of forced patriotism. Rudy Giuliani-ism, if you will. It's not earnest.
What the fuck is wrong with us? Why do we take these games so seriously?

The NFL is avoiding dealing with the problem, because there's so much frigging money on the table. Instead they shove yet more military might talk and fake patriotism down our throats, hoping we won't think too hard about it between rounds of beer.
We need to enjoy the victories and ignore the defeats.
key_insight: Enjoying football while knowing you'd never let your own children play it reveals a moral contradiction that implicates viewers in the harm done to players' bodies and brains.
